RNA-Seq De Novo TranscriptomicsPGCV - BDML Training Module
Home Contact Glossary
PGCV·BDML

Philippine Genome Center Visayas

Bioinformatics and Data Management Laboratory

TRAINING MODULE

RNA-Seq Analysis: De Novo Transcriptome Assembly

This simplified module is designed for limited-time RNA-seq demonstrations using a downsampled dataset. The main workflow focuses on quality control, fastp trimming, de novo assembly, assembly statistics, coding sequence prediction, quantification, functional annotation, and downstream DESeq2 analysis.

VERSION 1.0 · 01 JUL 2026

Backup Output Downloads Glossary
1

Development Note

Under development: This module is still being refined. For PGC Visayas use only. Not for reproduction, distribution, or external circulation without prior permission.
Before running commands: Always activate the correct conda environment first. If a command is not found, check that the environment containing the required bioinformatics tools is active.
2

Overview

Workflow: This version is optimized for limited time and a downsampled dataset. The main path focuses on the essential steps needed to complete the de novo workflow during the tutorial. Optional analyses can be run separately when there is enough time, computing resources, and a larger dataset.

2.1 Pre-processing

FASTQ files → FastQC and MultiQC → fastp → FastQC and MultiQC → Final cleaned reads

2.2 Main de novo workflow

Trinity → CD-HIT → Stats → ORF → Salmon → Trinotate
↓ optional paths

2.3 Optional sections

Rcorrector / SortMeRNA / Reference-based workflow
3

Directory Tree

Principle: A consistent directory structure makes the tutorial easier to follow and prevents input and output files from being mixed.

3.1 Guide directory tree

Show guide directory tree 
transcriptome_project/
├── fastq/
├── database/
├── 00_preprocess/
│   ├── qc/
│   ├── fastp/
│   ├── final_reads/
│   ├── rcorrector/       # optional
│   └── sortmerna/        # optional
├── 01_denovo/
│   ├── metadata.tsv
│   ├── trinity_samples.tsv
│   ├── trinity_out/
│   ├── transcriptome_filtered/
│   ├── assembly_stats/
│   ├── transdecoder/
│   ├── quantification/
│   └── trinotate/
├── 02_reference/         # optional
├── salmon/
│   ├── <sample>_quant.sf
│   └── deseq_inputs/
│       ├── count_matrix.csv
│       └── metadata.csv
└── downstream/
    └── trinity/

3.2 Start project directory

mkdir -p transcriptome_project
cd transcriptome_project
4

Dataset and Database Files

4.1 Create FASTQ directory

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p fastq
Dataset:  Download the RNA-seq training dataset here

After downloading, move the *.fastq.gz files into the fastq/ directory.

Show database setup

4.2 Create database directory

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p database
Databases:  Download the prepared database files here

After downloading, move the database files into the database/ directory.

5

Pre-processing: Quality Control and Filtering

5.1 FastQC and MultiQC

Principle: Assess raw sequencing quality before read cleaning or filtering.

Tool/s: FastQCMultiQC

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 00_preprocess/qc/raw_fastqc

fastqc fastq/*.fastq.gz \
    -o 00_preprocess/qc/raw_fastqc \
    -t 8

multiqc 00_preprocess/qc/raw_fastqc \
    -o 00_preprocess/qc/raw_multiqc
Check
  • 00_preprocess/qc/raw_fastqc/*.html
  • 00_preprocess/qc/raw_multiqc/multiqc_report.html
Reflect
Why run FastQC before trimming?

FastQC gives an overview of raw read quality, adapter content, and possible sequencing issues before any reads are modified.

Why use MultiQC?

MultiQC combines all FastQC reports into one summary, making it easier to compare samples.

5.2 Adapter Removal and Trimming

Principle: Remove adapters, sequencing artifacts, and low-quality bases. In this simplified demo, fastp is run directly on the raw FASTQ files to save time.

Tool/s: fastp

This option runs the command on one sample at a time. The same steps can then be repeated for the remaining samples.

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 00_preprocess/fastp

fastp \
    -i fastq/conditionA1_1.fastq.gz \
    -I fastq/conditionA1_2.fastq.gz \
    -o 00_preprocess/fastp/conditionA1_1.trim.fastq.gz \
    -O 00_preprocess/fastp/conditionA1_2.trim.fastq.gz \
    -h 00_preprocess/fastp/conditionA1.fastp.html \
    -j 00_preprocess/fastp/conditionA1.fastp.json \
    -q 30 \
    -w 16 \
    --detect_adapter_for_pe

Loop: run fastp for all samples

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 00_preprocess/fastp

for sample in conditionA1 conditionA2 conditionA3 conditionB1 conditionB2 conditionB3
do
    fastp \
        -i fastq/${sample}_1.fastq.gz \
        -I fastq/${sample}_2.fastq.gz \
        -o 00_preprocess/fastp/${sample}_1.trim.fastq.gz \
        -O 00_preprocess/fastp/${sample}_2.trim.fastq.gz \
        -h 00_preprocess/fastp/${sample}.fastp.html \
        -j 00_preprocess/fastp/${sample}.fastp.json \
        -q 30 \
        -w 16 \
        --detect_adapter_for_pe
done
Check
  • 00_preprocess/fastp/<sample>_1.trim.fastq.gz
  • 00_preprocess/fastp/<sample>_2.trim.fastq.gz
  • 00_preprocess/fastp/<sample>.fastp.html
  • 00_preprocess/fastp/<sample>.fastp.json
Reflect
Why check reads again after fastp?

This confirms whether trimming improved the reads and whether adapter or low-quality sequence content was reduced.

5.3 FastQC and MultiQC after fastp

Principle: Check the fastp-trimmed reads to confirm that read quality improved and adapter or low-quality sequence content was reduced.

Tool/s: FastQCMultiQC

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 00_preprocess/qc/trimmed_fastqc
mkdir -p 00_preprocess/qc/trimmed_multiqc

fastqc 00_preprocess/fastp/*.trim.fastq.gz \
    -o 00_preprocess/qc/trimmed_fastqc \
    -t 8

multiqc 00_preprocess/fastp 00_preprocess/qc/trimmed_fastqc \
    -o 00_preprocess/qc/trimmed_multiqc
Check
  • 00_preprocess/qc/trimmed_fastqc/*.html
  • 00_preprocess/qc/trimmed_multiqc/multiqc_report.html

5.4 Optional preprocessing steps

Optional: Rcorrector and rRNA removal can improve some analyses, but they add time and may require more resources. For this downsampled demo, these steps may be skipped.

Optional: Rcorrector k-mer correction

Principle: Correct probable sequencing errors using k-mer frequency patterns before trimming.

Tool/s: Rcorrector

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 00_preprocess/rcorrector

for sample in conditionA1 conditionA2 conditionA3 conditionB1 conditionB2 conditionB3
do
    run_rcorrector.pl \
        -1 fastq/${sample}_1.fastq.gz \
        -2 fastq/${sample}_2.fastq.gz \
        -k 31 \
        -t 16 \
        -od 00_preprocess/rcorrector
done
Important: If you use Rcorrector, adjust the fastp input paths to use 00_preprocess/rcorrector/<sample>_1.cor.fq.gz and 00_preprocess/rcorrector/<sample>_2.cor.fq.gz instead of the raw files in fastq/.
Optional: SortMeRNA rRNA removal

Principle: Remove ribosomal RNA reads to retain non-rRNA reads for downstream transcriptome analysis.

Resource note: SortMeRNA can require high memory, CPU time, and storage, especially when using large SILVA SSU and LSU reference databases. For limited-resource laptops or short tutorials, this step may be skipped.

Tool/s: SortMeRNA

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 00_preprocess/sortmerna

sortmerna \
    --ref database/SILVA_138.2_SSURef_tax_silva.fasta \
    --ref database/SILVA_138.2_LSURef_tax_silva.fasta \
    --reads 00_preprocess/fastp/conditionA1_1.trim.fastq.gz \
    --reads 00_preprocess/fastp/conditionA1_2.trim.fastq.gz \
    --paired_in \
    --fastx \
    --out2 \
    --other 00_preprocess/sortmerna/conditionA1.non_rRNA \
    --workdir 00_preprocess/sortmerna/sortmerna_conditionA1 \
    --threads 16
Important: If SortMeRNA is skipped, prepare final reads from the fastp-trimmed reads. If SortMeRNA is performed, prepare final reads from the non-rRNA outputs.

5.5 Prepare Final Reads

Principle: Standardize the final read names so the same files can be used by Trinity and the downstream de novo workflow.

Main demo path: use fastp-trimmed reads

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 00_preprocess/final_reads

for sample in conditionA1 conditionA2 conditionA3 conditionB1 conditionB2 conditionB3
do
    cp 00_preprocess/fastp/${sample}_1.trim.fastq.gz \
        00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_1.final.fastq.gz

    cp 00_preprocess/fastp/${sample}_2.trim.fastq.gz \
        00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_2.final.fastq.gz
done
Show final-read preparation after SortMeRNA
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 00_preprocess/final_reads

for sample in conditionA1 conditionA2 conditionA3 conditionB1 conditionB2 conditionB3
do
    gzip -c 00_preprocess/sortmerna/${sample}.non_rRNA_fwd.fq \
        > 00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_1.final.fastq.gz

    gzip -c 00_preprocess/sortmerna/${sample}.non_rRNA_rev.fq \
        > 00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_2.final.fastq.gz
done
Check
  • 00_preprocess/final_reads/<sample>_1.final.fastq.gz
  • 00_preprocess/final_reads/<sample>_2.final.fastq.gz
6

De novo Workflow

Principle: De novo transcriptome assembly reconstructs transcript sequences directly from RNA-seq reads without using a reference genome.

6.1 De novo directory guide

Click here to view the de novo directory guide.
Run 
01_denovo/
├── metadata.tsv
├── trinity_samples.tsv
├── trinity_out/
│   └── Trinity.fasta
├── transcriptome_filtered/
│   ├── trinity_cdhit98.fasta
│   ├── trinity_cdhit98.fasta.clstr
│   └── trinity_cdhit98.gene_trans_map.tsv
├── assembly_stats/
│   └── trinity_cdhit98_stats.txt
├── transdecoder/
│   ├── *.transdecoder.pep
│   ├── *.transdecoder.cds
│   └── *.transdecoder.gff3
├── quantification/
│   ├── trinity_salmon_index/
│   └── <sample>/
│       ├── quant.sf
│       └── aux_info/
└── trinotate/
    ├── uniprot.blastp.outfmt6
    ├── uniprot.blastx.outfmt6
    ├── pfam.domtblout
    └── trinotate_annotation_report.xls

6.2 Trinity Assembly

Tool/s: Trinity

Create metadata file for Trinity using the final cleaned reads

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 01_denovo

cat > 01_denovo/metadata.tsv <<'EOF'
sample	condition	replicate	forward_reads	reverse_reads
conditionA1	conditionA	1	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA1_1.final.fastq.gz	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA1_2.final.fastq.gz
conditionA2	conditionA	2	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA2_1.final.fastq.gz	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA2_2.final.fastq.gz
conditionA3	conditionA	3	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA3_1.final.fastq.gz	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA3_2.final.fastq.gz
conditionB1	conditionB	1	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionB1_1.final.fastq.gz	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionB1_2.final.fastq.gz
conditionB2	conditionB	2	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionB2_1.final.fastq.gz	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionB2_2.final.fastq.gz
conditionB3	conditionB	3	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionB3_1.final.fastq.gz	00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionB3_2.final.fastq.gz
EOF

awk 'BEGIN{FS=OFS="	"} NR>1 {print $2, $1, $4, $5}' \
    01_denovo/metadata.tsv \
    > 01_denovo/trinity_samples.tsv

cat 01_denovo/trinity_samples.tsv
Check
  • 01_denovo/metadata.tsv
  • 01_denovo/trinity_samples.tsv

Run Trinity using the sample file

Runtime note: Trinity can take approximately 120 minutes for this tutorial dataset, depending on read depth, CPU allocation, memory, and server load.

cd transcriptome_project

Trinity \
    --seqType fq \
    --samples_file 01_denovo/trinity_samples.tsv \
    --max_memory 50G \
    --CPU 20 \
    --full_cleanup \
    --output 01_denovo/trinity_out
Check
  • 01_denovo/trinity_out.Trinity.fasta
  • 01_denovo/trinity_out.Trinity.fasta.gene_trans_map.tsv
Reflect
What is the main purpose of Trinity?

Trinity performs de novo transcriptome assembly, meaning it reconstructs transcript sequences from RNA-seq reads without requiring a reference genome.

What is the main output of Trinity?

The main output is a transcriptome assembly file, usually named Trinity.fasta, which contains assembled transcript sequences.

6.3 CD-HIT Clustering

Principle: Cluster highly similar transcript sequences to reduce redundancy in the transcriptome assembly.

Tool/s: CD-HIT-EST

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered

cd-hit-est \
    -i 01_denovo/trinity_out.Trinity.fasta \
    -o 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta \
    -c 0.98 \
    -n 10 \
    -T 24 \
    -M 0

grep "^>" 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta \
    | sed 's/^>//' \
    | awk '{print $1 "	" $1}' \
    > 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.gene_trans_map.tsv
Check
  • 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta
  • 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta.clstr
  • 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.gene_trans_map.tsv
Reflect
Why is CD-HIT used after transcriptome assembly?

CD-HIT reduces redundancy by clustering highly similar transcript sequences. This can simplify the assembly and reduce duplicate or nearly identical transcripts. Check CD-HIT parameters before deciding the clustering threshold.

6.4 Assembly Statistics

Principle: Assembly statistics summarize transcript number, total assembled bases, transcript length distribution, and N50. These values help describe the assembly but should not be used alone to judge quality.

Tool/s: TrinityStats

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 01_denovo/assembly_stats

TrinityStats.pl \
    01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta \
    > 01_denovo/assembly_stats/trinity_cdhit98_stats.txt

cat 01_denovo/assembly_stats/trinity_cdhit98_stats.txt
Check
  • 01_denovo/assembly_stats/trinity_cdhit98_stats.txt
Optional: run Bowtie2 read mapping to assess read representation.

Principle: Map the final cleaned reads back to the assembled transcriptome to estimate how well the assembly represents the input reads.

Tool/s: Bowtie2

Optional step: This step is useful for assembly evaluation, but it was not included in the main hands-on workflow to save time. For a short demo, you may skip this and only review the example mapping output below.

Build Bowtie2 index from the assembled transcriptome

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 01_denovo/assembly_stats/bowtie2

bowtie2-build 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta 01_denovo/assembly_stats/bowtie2/trinity_cdhit98

Map final cleaned reads back to the assembly

cd transcriptome_project

for sample in conditionA1 conditionA2 conditionA3 conditionB1 conditionB2 conditionB3
do
    bowtie2 -p 16 -x 01_denovo/assembly_stats/bowtie2/trinity_cdhit98 -1 00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_1.final.fastq.gz -2 00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_2.final.fastq.gz -S /dev/null 2> 01_denovo/assembly_stats/bowtie2/${sample}.bowtie2.log
done

Summarize Bowtie2 overall alignment rates

cd transcriptome_project

grep "overall alignment rate" 01_denovo/assembly_stats/bowtie2/*.bowtie2.log
Check
  • 01_denovo/assembly_stats/bowtie2/*.bt2
  • 01_denovo/assembly_stats/bowtie2/<sample>.bowtie2.log
Interpretation: A higher overall alignment rate suggests that many reads are represented in the assembly. However, this does not prove that the assembly is complete or error-free. It should be interpreted together with assembly statistics and biological expectations.

Note: Bowtie2 read-representation mapping was not included in the main shortened run, but an example is shown here so participants can see how this output may look in a full analysis.

Example TrinityStats output from a larger dataset

################################
## Counts of transcripts, etc.
################################
Total trinity 'genes':	124385
Total trinity transcripts:	188223
Percent GC: 45.04

########################################
Stats based on ALL transcript contigs:
########################################

	Contig N10: 5790
	Contig N20: 4316
	Contig N30: 3465
	Contig N40: 2829
	Contig N50: 2290

	Median contig length: 535
	Average contig: 1148.83
	Total assembled bases: 216235336


#####################################################
## Stats based on ONLY LONGEST ISOFORM per 'GENE':
#####################################################

	Contig N10: 5387
	Contig N20: 3936
	Contig N30: 3066
	Contig N40: 2351
	Contig N50: 1719

	Median contig length: 384
	Average contig: 827.25
	Total assembled bases: 102897590

Example Bowtie2 read-representation mapping output

1000000 reads; of these:
  1000000 (100.00%) were paired; of these:
    120000 (12.00%) aligned concordantly 0 times
    760000 (76.00%) aligned concordantly exactly 1 time
    120000 (12.00%) aligned concordantly >1 times
88.00% overall alignment rate

Interpretation note: These are example values only. Actual results depend on organism, dataset size, read quality, read depth, assembly quality, and parameter choices.

Reflect
Is a higher N50 always better?

No. A higher N50 means the assembly has longer contigs or transcripts, but it does not always mean the assembly is more accurate. Very high N50 can also result from misassembled or chimeric transcripts.

What other metrics should be checked aside from N50?

Check the number of transcripts, total assembled bases, read mapping rate, transcript redundancy, and possible contamination.

6.5 Open Reading Frame Prediction

Principle: Identify likely protein-coding regions from assembled transcript sequences before quantification and functional annotation.

Tool/s: TransDecoder

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 01_denovo/transdecoder

TransDecoder.LongOrfs \
    -t 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta \
    --output_dir 01_denovo/transdecoder

TransDecoder.Predict \
    -t 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta \
    --single_best_only \
    --output_dir 01_denovo/transdecoder
Check
  • 01_denovo/transdecoder/*.transdecoder.pep
  • 01_denovo/transdecoder/*.transdecoder.cds
  • 01_denovo/transdecoder/*.transdecoder.gff3
What do the TransDecoder output files mean?
  • .pep contains the predicted protein sequences translated from likely coding regions.
  • .cds contains the nucleotide coding sequences corresponding to the predicted proteins.
  • .gff3 contains the coordinates and feature annotations of the predicted coding regions.
Reflect
Does every assembled transcript contain a valid ORF?

No. Some transcripts may be non-coding, incomplete, or truncated. ORF prediction helps separate likely coding transcripts from non-coding or incomplete sequences.

6.6 Quantification for Expression Analysis

Principle: Estimate transcript abundance by assigning reads to transcript sequences.

Tool/s: Salmon

This option runs the command on one sample at a time. The same steps can then be repeated for the remaining samples.

cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 01_denovo/quantification

salmon index \
    -t 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta \
    -i 01_denovo/quantification/trinity_salmon_index \
    -k 31

salmon quant \
    -i 01_denovo/quantification/trinity_salmon_index \
    -l A \
    -1 00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA1_1.final.fastq.gz \
    -2 00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA1_2.final.fastq.gz \
    --validateMappings \
    --gcBias \
    --seqBias \
    -p 16 \
    -o 01_denovo/quantification/conditionA1
Show loop: quantify all samples
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 01_denovo/quantification

salmon index \
    -t 01_denovo/transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta \
    -i 01_denovo/quantification/trinity_salmon_index \
    -k 31

for sample in conditionA1 conditionA2 conditionA3 conditionB1 conditionB2 conditionB3
do
    salmon quant \
        -i 01_denovo/quantification/trinity_salmon_index \
        -l A \
        -1 00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_1.final.fastq.gz \
        -2 00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_2.final.fastq.gz \
        --validateMappings \
        --gcBias \
        --seqBias \
        -p 16 \
        -o 01_denovo/quantification/${sample}
done
Check
  • 01_denovo/quantification/<sample>/quant.sf
  • 01_denovo/quantification/<sample>/aux_info/
Show example Salmon quant.sf output and columns
Note: Salmon produces one quant.sf file per sample. The values below are example values only and are shown to help interpret the output format.

Example quant.sf output

Name	Length	EffectiveLength	TPM	NumReads
TRINITY_DN1000_c0_g1_i1	1800	1551.32	25.421	345.000
TRINITY_DN1001_c0_g1_i1	950	701.85	10.337	89.000
TRINITY_DN1002_c0_g2_i1	2400	2150.44	0.000	0.000
TRINITY_DN1003_c1_g1_i2	1250	1001.67	5.782	47.000
TRINITY_DN1004_c0_g1_i1	3200	2950.21	120.456	2450.000

Column meanings

ColumnMeaningHow to interpret
NameTranscript identifier from the transcriptome FASTA file.Each row corresponds to one transcript sequence in the assembly.
LengthOriginal transcript length in base pairs.Longer transcripts generally have more possible positions where reads can align.
EffectiveLengthAdjusted transcript length used by Salmon for abundance estimation.This accounts for fragment length and bias correction; it is usually shorter than the original transcript length.
TPMTranscripts Per Million.A normalized abundance value useful for comparing relative transcript expression within or across samples, with caution.
NumReadsEstimated number of reads or fragments assigned to the transcript.This is an estimated count, not always a whole-number raw count. It is commonly used for downstream count-based summaries.
Important: TPM and NumReads are not the same. TPM is normalized abundance, while NumReads is the estimated read or fragment support for each transcript.
Reflect
What is the purpose of Salmon in transcriptome analysis?

Salmon estimates transcript abundance by quantifying how many reads are assigned to each transcript.

What common output values does Salmon provide?

Salmon provides abundance estimates such as TPM and estimated counts. TPM is useful for comparing relative transcript expression within or across samples.

6.7 Functional Annotation with Trinotate

Alternative option: Trinotate can also be accessed through Galaxy Europe: https://usegalaxy.eu/root?tool_id=trinotate. This can be useful when local installation or database setup is difficult.

Principle: Functional annotation connects predicted coding sequences to known proteins, domains, gene ontology terms, and possible biological functions.

Tool/s: DIAMONDHMMER/PfamTrinotate

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 01_denovo/trinotate
cd 01_denovo/trinotate

diamond blastp \
    --query ../transdecoder/trinity_cdhit98.fasta.transdecoder.pep \
    --db ../../database/uniprot.dmnd \
    --out uniprot.blastp.outfmt6 \
    --outfmt 6 \
    --evalue 1e-20 \
    --max-target-seqs 1 \
    --threads 24

diamond blastx \
    --query ../transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta \
    --db ../../database/uniprot.dmnd \
    --out uniprot.blastx.outfmt6 \
    --outfmt 6 \
    --evalue 1e-20 \
    --max-target-seqs 1 \
    --threads 24

hmmscan \
    --cpu 24 \
    --domtblout pfam.domtblout \
    ../../database/Pfam-A.hmm \
    ../transdecoder/trinity_cdhit98.fasta.transdecoder.pep \
    > pfam.hmmscan.log

# Download the Trinotate SQLite database if not already present

Trinotate Trinotate.sqlite init \
    --gene_trans_map ../transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.gene_trans_map.tsv \
    --transcript_fasta ../transcriptome_filtered/trinity_cdhit98.fasta \
    --transdecoder_pep ../transdecoder/trinity_cdhit98.fasta.transdecoder.pep

# Load output

Trinotate Trinotate.sqlite --LOAD_swissprot_blastp uniprot.blastp.outfmt6

Trinotate Trinotate.sqlite --LOAD_swissprot_blastx uniprot.blastx.outfmt6

Trinotate Trinotate.sqlite --LOAD_pfam pfam.domtblout

Trinotate Trinotate.sqlite report > trinotate_annotation_report.xls
Check
  • 01_denovo/trinotate/trinotate_annotation_report.xls final annotation report
  • 01_denovo/trinotate/uniprot.blastp.outfmt6 BLASTP result from predicted proteins
  • 01_denovo/trinotate/uniprot.blastx.outfmt6 BLASTX result from transcript sequences
  • 01_denovo/trinotate/pfam.domtblout Pfam protein-domain search result
Reflect
What is the purpose of Trinotate?

Trinotate is used to functionally annotate assembled transcripts by combining evidence from sequence similarity searches, predicted protein domains, and annotation databases into one report.

Why is annotation important after transcriptome assembly?

Assembly produces transcript sequences, but it does not explain what those transcripts are. Annotation helps assign possible gene names, protein functions, domains, and biological roles to the assembled transcripts.

7

Optional Reference-based Workflow

Optional for this demo: The reference-based workflow is hidden because it can be difficult to complete using a heavily downsampled dataset. For a short workshop, focus on the de novo workflow first.
Show optional reference-based workflow
8

Reference-based Workflow

Principle: Reference-based transcriptomics maps RNA-seq reads to a reference genome and uses the alignments to assemble or quantify transcripts.

8.1 Reference-based directory guide

Show reference-based directory guide
Run 
02_reference/
├── alignments/
│   ├── <sample>.sorted.bam
│   └── <sample>.sorted.bam.bai
├── stringtie/
│   ├── <sample>.gtf
│   └── stringtie_merge_list.txt
├── merged/
│   ├── stringtie_merged.gtf
│   ├── stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa
│   └── stringtie.gene_trans_map.tsv
├── transdecoder/
│   ├── *.transdecoder.pep
│   ├── *.transdecoder.cds
│   └── *.transdecoder.gff3
├── quantification/
│   ├── stringtie_salmon_index/
│   └── <sample>/
│       ├── quant.sf
│       └── aux_info/
└── trinotate/
    ├── uniprot.blastp.outfmt6
    ├── nr.blastp.outfmt6
    ├── pfam.domtblout
    ├── trinotate_annotation_report.xls
    └── GO_annotations.txt

8.2 Download Reference and Build HISAT2 Index

Principle: A reference genome and index allow RNA-seq reads to be mapped to genomic coordinates.

Tool/s: HISAT2

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p reference/chanos_chanos

wget -P reference/chanos_chanos \
    https://ftp.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/genomes/refseq/vertebrate_other/Chanos_chanos/reference/GCF_902362185.1_fChaCha1.1/GCF_902362185.1_fChaCha1.1_genomic.fna.gz

wget -P reference/chanos_chanos \
    https://ftp.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/genomes/refseq/vertebrate_other/Chanos_chanos/reference/GCF_902362185.1_fChaCha1.1/GCF_902362185.1_fChaCha1.1_genomic.gtf.gz

gunzip reference/chanos_chanos/*.gz

mkdir -p reference/chanos_chanos/reference_hisat2_index

hisat2-build \
    reference/chanos_chanos/GCF_902362185.1_fChaCha1.1_genomic.fna \
    reference/chanos_chanos/reference_hisat2_index/chanos_chanos
Check
  • reference/chanos_chanos/*.fna
  • reference/chanos_chanos/*.gtf
  • reference/chanos_chanos/reference_hisat2_index/chanos_chanos.*.ht2

8.3 HISAT2 Read Mapping

Principle: Map RNA-seq reads to the reference genome to produce coordinate-sorted BAM files.

Tool/s: HISAT2Samtools

Run one sample, then repeat across all samples

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 02_reference/alignments

hisat2 \
    -p 24 \
    -x reference/chanos_chanos/reference_hisat2_index/chanos_chanos \
    -1 00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA1_1.final.fastq.gz \
    -2 00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA1_2.final.fastq.gz \
    | samtools sort -@ 8 \
        -o 02_reference/alignments/conditionA1.sorted.bam

samtools index 02_reference/alignments/conditionA1.sorted.bam
Show loop: map all samples
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 02_reference/alignments

for sample in conditionA1 conditionA2 conditionA3 conditionB1 conditionB2 conditionB3
do
    hisat2 \
        -p 24 \
        -x reference/chanos_chanos/reference_hisat2_index/chanos_chanos \
        -1 00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_1.final.fastq.gz \
        -2 00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_2.final.fastq.gz \
        | samtools sort -@ 8 \
            -o 02_reference/alignments/${sample}.sorted.bam

    samtools index 02_reference/alignments/${sample}.sorted.bam
done
Check
  • 02_reference/alignments/<sample>.sorted.bam
  • 02_reference/alignments/<sample>.sorted.bam.bai

8.4 StringTie Reference-based Assembly

Principle: Assemble transcripts from mapped reads and merge sample-level transcript models into one reference-guided transcript set.

Tool/s: StringTiegffread

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

awk 'BEGIN{FS=OFS="	"} 
    /^#/ {print; next} 
    ($3=="transcript" || $3=="exon") && $9 ~ /gene_id "[^"]+"/ && $9 ~ /transcript_id "[^"]+"/ {print}
' reference/chanos_chanos/GCF_902362185.1_fChaCha1.1_genomic.gtf \
    > reference/chanos_chanos/GCF_902362185.1_fChaCha1.1_genomic.stringtie.gtf

mkdir -p 02_reference/stringtie

for sample in conditionA1 conditionA2 conditionA3 conditionB1 conditionB2 conditionB3
do
    stringtie \
        02_reference/alignments/${sample}.sorted.bam \
        -p 24 \
        -G reference/chanos_chanos/GCF_902362185.1_fChaCha1.1_genomic.stringtie.gtf \
        -o 02_reference/stringtie/${sample}.gtf
done

mkdir -p 02_reference/merged

ls 02_reference/stringtie/*.gtf \
    > 02_reference/stringtie/stringtie_merge_list.txt

stringtie --merge \
    -p 24 \
    -G reference/chanos_chanos/GCF_902362185.1_fChaCha1.1_genomic.stringtie.gtf \
    -o 02_reference/merged/stringtie_merged.gtf \
    02_reference/stringtie/stringtie_merge_list.txt

gffread \
    02_reference/merged/stringtie_merged.gtf \
    -g reference/chanos_chanos/GCF_902362185.1_fChaCha1.1_genomic.fna \
    -w 02_reference/merged/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa

awk 'BEGIN{FS=OFS="	"} 
    $3=="transcript" && /gene_id "/ && /transcript_id "/ {
        gene=$0; tx=$0;
        sub(/^.*gene_id "/, "", gene); sub(/".*$/, "", gene);
        sub(/^.*transcript_id "/, "", tx); sub(/".*$/, "", tx);
        print gene, tx
    }
' 02_reference/merged/stringtie_merged.gtf \
    > 02_reference/merged/stringtie.gene_trans_map.tsv
Check
  • 02_reference/stringtie/<sample>.gtf
  • 02_reference/merged/stringtie_merged.gtf
  • 02_reference/merged/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa
  • 02_reference/merged/stringtie.gene_trans_map.tsv

8.5 Open Reading Frame Prediction

Principle: Identify likely protein-coding regions from the reference-guided transcript sequences before quantification and functional annotation.

Tool/s: TransDecoder

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 02_reference/transdecoder

TransDecoder.LongOrfs \
    -t 02_reference/merged/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa \
    --output_dir 02_reference/transdecoder

TransDecoder.Predict \
    -t 02_reference/merged/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa \
    --single_best_only \
    --output_dir 02_reference/transdecoder
Check
  • 02_reference/transdecoder/*.transdecoder.pep
  • 02_reference/transdecoder/*.transdecoder.cds
  • 02_reference/transdecoder/*.transdecoder.gff3
Reflect
Does every assembled transcript contain a valid ORF?

No. Some transcripts may be non-coding, incomplete, fragmented, or assembly artifacts. ORF prediction helps separate likely coding transcripts from non-coding or incomplete sequences.

8.6 Quantification for Expression Analysis

Principle: Estimate transcript abundance against the StringTie reference-guided transcriptome.

Tool/s: Salmon

This option runs the command on one sample at a time. The same steps can then be repeated for the remaining samples.

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 02_reference/quantification

salmon index \
    -t 02_reference/merged/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa \
    -i 02_reference/quantification/stringtie_salmon_index \
    -k 31

salmon quant \
    -i 02_reference/quantification/stringtie_salmon_index \
    -l A \
    -1 00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA1_1.final.fastq.gz \
    -2 00_preprocess/final_reads/conditionA1_2.final.fastq.gz \
    --validateMappings \
    --gcBias \
    --seqBias \
    -p 16 \
    -o 02_reference/quantification/conditionA1
Show loop: quantify all samples
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 02_reference/quantification

salmon index \
    -t 02_reference/merged/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa \
    -i 02_reference/quantification/stringtie_salmon_index \
    -k 31

for sample in conditionA1 conditionA2 conditionA3 conditionB1 conditionB2 conditionB3
do
    salmon quant \
        -i 02_reference/quantification/stringtie_salmon_index \
        -l A \
        -1 00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_1.final.fastq.gz \
        -2 00_preprocess/final_reads/${sample}_2.final.fastq.gz \
        --validateMappings \
        --gcBias \
        --seqBias \
        -p 16 \
        -o 02_reference/quantification/${sample}
done
Check
  • 02_reference/quantification/<sample>/quant.sf
  • 02_reference/quantification/<sample>/aux_info/
Reflect
What is the purpose of Salmon in transcriptome analysis?

Salmon estimates transcript abundance by quantifying how many reads are assigned to each transcript.

What common output values does Salmon provide?

Salmon provides abundance estimates such as TPM and estimated counts. TPM is useful for comparing relative transcript expression within or across samples.

8.7 Functional Annotation with Trinotate

Principle: Functional annotation connects predicted coding sequences to known proteins, domains, gene ontology terms, and possible biological functions.

Tool/s: DIAMONDHMMER/PfamTrinotate

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p 02_reference/trinotate
cd 02_reference/trinotate

diamond blastp \
    --query ../transdecoder/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa.transdecoder.pep \
    --db ../../database/uniprot_sprot.dmnd \
    --out uniprot.blastp.outfmt6 \
    --outfmt 6 \
    --evalue 1e-20 \
    --max-target-seqs 1 \
    --threads 24

diamond blastp \
    --query ../transdecoder/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa.transdecoder.pep \
    --db ../../database/nr.dmnd \
    --out nr.blastp.outfmt6 \
    --outfmt 6 qseqid sseqid pident length mismatch gapopen qstart qend sstart send evalue bitscore stitle \
    --evalue 1e-20 \
    --max-target-seqs 1 \
    --threads 24

hmmpress -f ../../database/Pfam-A.hmm

hmmscan \
    --cpu 24 \
    --domtblout pfam.domtblout \
    ../../database/Pfam-A.hmm \
    ../transdecoder/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa.transdecoder.pep \
    > pfam.hmmscan.log

Build_Trinotate_Boilerplate_SQLite_db.pl Trinotate

Trinotate Trinotate.sqlite init \
    --gene_trans_map ../merged/stringtie.gene_trans_map.tsv \
    --transcript_fasta ../merged/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa \
    --transdecoder_pep ../transdecoder/stringtie_merged.transcripts.fa.transdecoder.pep

Trinotate Trinotate.sqlite LOAD_swissprot_blastp uniprot.blastp.outfmt6

Trinotate Trinotate.sqlite LOAD_custom_blast \
    --outfmt6 nr.blastp.outfmt6 \
    --prog blastp \
    --dbtype nr

Trinotate Trinotate.sqlite LOAD_pfam pfam.domtblout

Trinotate Trinotate.sqlite report \
    > trinotate_annotation_report.xls

extract_GO_assignments_from_Trinotate_xls.pl \
    --Trinotate_xls trinotate_annotation_report.xls \
    --gene \
    --include_ancestral_terms \
    > GO_annotations.txt
Check
  • 02_reference/trinotate/trinotate_annotation_report.xls
  • 02_reference/trinotate/GO_annotations.txt
  • 02_reference/trinotate/uniprot.blastp.outfmt6
  • 02_reference/trinotate/nr.blastp.outfmt6
  • 02_reference/trinotate/pfam.domtblout
Reflect
What is the purpose of Trinotate?

Trinotate annotates assembled transcripts by integrating similarity searches and protein-domain evidence into one annotation report.

Why is annotation important after assembly?

Assembly only gives transcript sequences. Annotation helps interpret what those transcripts may do biologically.

9

Salmon to DESeq2 and Downstream Analysis

Principle: Salmon produces transcript-level abundance estimates. The next step converts quant.sf files into a count matrix and sample metadata that can be used by DESeq2 for exploratory plots and differential-expression testing.

Input location: This section expects Salmon outputs from the de novo workflow at 01_denovo/quantification/<sample>/quant.sf. If the optional reference workflow is used instead, replace the source path with 02_reference/quantification/<sample>/quant.sf.

Main output locations:

Check
  • salmon/<sample>_quant.sf flat Salmon files used by the converter script
  • salmon/deseq_inputs/count_matrix.csv rounded count matrix for DESeq2
  • salmon/deseq_inputs/metadata.csv sample metadata inferred from sample names
  • downstream/trinity/ DESeq2 tables, PCA, sample-distance heatmap, MA plot, volcano plot, and DEG heatmap

9.1 Prepare folders and flatten Salmon outputs

Principle: The converter script searches for files ending in _quant.sf directly inside the salmon/ folder. The command below copies each per-sample Salmon output into that expected flat format.

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

mkdir -p salmon scripts downstream/trinity

for sample in conditionA1 conditionA2 conditionA3 conditionB1 conditionB2 conditionB3
do
    cp 01_denovo/quantification/${sample}/quant.sf         salmon/${sample}_quant.sf
done

ls -lh salmon/*_quant.sf
Reference-based alternative: If you quantified against the optional reference-guided transcriptome, change 01_denovo/quantification/${sample}/quant.sf to 02_reference/quantification/${sample}/quant.sf.
Check
  • salmon/conditionA1_quant.sf
  • salmon/conditionA2_quant.sf
  • salmon/conditionA3_quant.sf
  • salmon/conditionB1_quant.sf
  • salmon/conditionB2_quant.sf
  • salmon/conditionB3_quant.sf
B. Create the Salmon-to-DESeq2 conversion script

Principle: This script reads each Salmon quant.sf file, extracts NumReads, rounds the estimated counts, and writes a sample-by-feature count matrix plus metadata.

mkdir -p scripts

cat > scripts/salmon_to_deseq2.R <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env Rscript

args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)

usage <- function() {
  cat(
    "Usage:\n",
    "  Rscript make_salmon_deseq_inputs.R [output_dir]\n\n",
    "Example:\n",
    "  Rscript make_salmon_deseq_inputs.R salmon/deseq_inputs\n",
    sep = ""
  )
}

if (length(args) > 1 || any(args %in% c("-h", "--help"))) {
  usage()
  quit(status = ifelse(length(args) == 1, 0, 1))
}

salmon_root <- "salmon"
if (!dir.exists(salmon_root)) {
  stop("Missing input folder: ", salmon_root, call. = FALSE)
}

salmon_root <- normalizePath(salmon_root, mustWork = TRUE)
output_dir <- if (length(args) == 1) args[[1]] else file.path(salmon_root, "deseq_inputs")
dir.create(output_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

quant_files <- list.files(
  salmon_root,
  pattern = "_quant\\.sf$",
  full.names = TRUE
)

if (length(quant_files) == 0) {
  stop("No quant.sf files found under: ", salmon_root, call. = FALSE)
}

sample_from_quant <- function(path) {
  sub("_quant\\.sf$", "", basename(path))
}

format_sample_id <- function(sample) {
  sample <- sub("^condition([A-Za-z]+)([0-9]+)$", "condition\\1_\\2", sample)
  sample <- sub("^([A-Za-z]+)([0-9]+)$", "\\1_\\2", sample)
  sample
}

treatment_from_sample <- function(sample_id) {
  if (grepl("_[0-9]+$", sample_id)) {
    sub("_[0-9]+$", "", sample_id)
  } else if (grepl("[0-9]+$", sample_id)) {
    sub("[0-9]+$", "", sample_id)
  } else {
    sample_id
  }
}

replicate_from_sample <- function(sample_id) {
  if (grepl("[0-9]+$", sample_id)) {
    sub("^.*?([0-9]+)$", "\\1", sample_id)
  } else {
    NA_character_
  }
}

read_quant_counts <- function(path) {
  dat <- read.delim(path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE)
  required <- c("Name", "NumReads")
  missing <- setdiff(required, colnames(dat))
  if (length(missing) > 0) {
    stop("Missing columns in ", path, ": ", paste(missing, collapse = ", "), call. = FALSE)
  }
  counts <- round(dat$NumReads)
  names(counts) <- dat$Name
  counts
}

sample_raw <- vapply(quant_files, sample_from_quant, character(1))
sample_ids <- vapply(sample_raw, format_sample_id, character(1))
ord <- order(sample_ids)
quant_files <- quant_files[ord]
sample_ids <- sample_ids[ord]

count_vectors <- lapply(quant_files, read_quant_counts)
all_features <- sort(unique(unlist(lapply(count_vectors, names))))

count_matrix <- matrix(
  0L,
  nrow = length(all_features),
  ncol = length(count_vectors),
  dimnames = list(all_features, sample_ids)
)

for (i in seq_along(count_vectors)) {
  current <- count_vectors[[i]]
  count_matrix[names(current), i] <- as.integer(current)
}

count_output <- data.frame(gene_id = rownames(count_matrix), count_matrix, check.names = FALSE)
write.csv(
  count_output,
  file = file.path(output_dir, "count_matrix.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE,
  quote = FALSE
)

metadata <- data.frame(
  Sample = sample_ids,
  Treatment = vapply(sample_ids, treatment_from_sample, character(1)),
  Replicate = vapply(sample_ids, replicate_from_sample, character(1)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

write.csv(
  metadata,
  file = file.path(output_dir, "metadata.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE,
  quote = FALSE
)

cat("Wrote:\n")
cat("  ", file.path(output_dir, "count_matrix.csv"), "\n", sep = "")
cat("  ", file.path(output_dir, "metadata.csv"), "\n", sep = "")
EOF
Check
  • scripts/salmon_to_deseq2.R

9.2 Run Salmon-to-DESeq2 conversion

Principle: The output directory can be supplied as a command-line argument. If no argument is given, the script writes to salmon/deseq_inputs/.

Run 
cd transcriptome_project

Rscript scripts/salmon_to_deseq2.R salmon/deseq_inputs
Check
  • salmon/deseq_inputs/count_matrix.csv
  • salmon/deseq_inputs/metadata.csv

9.3 Check the DESeq2 input files

Principle: Before running DESeq2, confirm that the count-matrix column names match the metadata sample names.

cd transcriptome_project

head salmon/deseq_inputs/count_matrix.csv
cat salmon/deseq_inputs/metadata.csv
Expected metadata format: For a sample named conditionA1, the script formats the sample ID as conditionA_1, assigns Treatment = conditionA, and assigns Replicate = 1.

9.4 Create the downstream R Markdown file

Principle: The downstream file uses salmon/deseq_inputs/count_matrix.csv and salmon/deseq_inputs/metadata.csv as inputs, then saves exploratory and differential-expression outputs under downstream/trinity/.

Show command to create scripts/downstream.Rmd 
mkdir -p scripts

cat > scripts/downstream.Rmd <<'EOF'
---
title: "Transcriptome Downstream Analysis"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
ensure_packages <- function(packages, bioc = character(0)) {
  missing_cran <- packages[!vapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE, FUN.VALUE = logical(1))]
  if (length(missing_cran) > 0) {
    install.packages(missing_cran, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
  }

  if (length(bioc) > 0) {
    if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
      install.packages("BiocManager", repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
    }
    missing_bioc <- bioc[!vapply(bioc, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE, FUN.VALUE = logical(1))]
    if (length(missing_bioc) > 0) {
      BiocManager::install(missing_bioc, ask = FALSE, update = FALSE)
    }
  }
}

ensure_packages(
  packages = c("ggplot2", "pheatmap", "RColorBrewer", "circlize"),
  bioc = c("DESeq2", "ComplexHeatmap")
)

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(DESeq2)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(pheatmap)
  library(RColorBrewer)
  library(ComplexHeatmap)
  library(circlize)
})

count_matrix_path <- "salmon/deseq_inputs/count_matrix.csv"
metadata_path <- "salmon/deseq_inputs/metadata.csv"
output_dir <- "downstream/trinity"
baseline <- "conditionA"
contrast_group <- "conditionB"
salmon_root <- "salmon"
ma_sig_color <- "darkred"
ma_nonsig_color <- "gray50"
volcano_up_color <- "darkblue"
volcano_down_color <- "darkred"
volcano_nonsig_color <- "gray50"
transparent_theme <- theme(
  plot.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = NA),
  panel.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = NA),
  legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = NA),
  legend.box.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = NA)
)
```

```{r}
dir.create(output_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
quant_files <- list.files(salmon_root, pattern = "_quant\\.sf$", full.names = TRUE)
stopifnot(length(quant_files) > 0)
```


```{r input}
counts <- read.csv(count_matrix_path, row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)
metadata <- read.csv(metadata_path, row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)

counts <- round(as.matrix(counts))
storage.mode(counts) <- "integer"
metadata <- metadata[colnames(counts), , drop = FALSE]
metadata$Treatment <- factor(metadata$Treatment, levels = c(baseline, setdiff(unique(metadata$Treatment), baseline)))
stopifnot(all(colnames(counts) == rownames(metadata)))
```

```{r deseq}
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = counts, colData = metadata, design = ~ Treatment)
dds <- dds[rowSums(counts(dds) >= 10) >= min(table(metadata$Treatment)), ]
dds <- estimateSizeFactors(dds)
dds
```

```{r pca}
vsd <- vst(dds, blind = TRUE)
pca_df <- plotPCA(vsd, intgroup = "Treatment", returnData = TRUE)
percent_var <- round(100 * attr(pca_df, "percentVar"))

pca_plot <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(PC1, PC2, color = Treatment, label = name)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(vjust = -0.8, size = 3) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "PCA", x = paste0("PC1: ", percent_var[1], "% variance"), y = paste0("PC2: ", percent_var[2], "% variance")) +
  transparent_theme

ggsave(file.path(output_dir, "PCA_plot.png"), pca_plot, width = 6, height = 5, dpi = 300, bg = "transparent")
pca_plot
```

```{r sample-distance}
rld <- rlog(dds, blind = TRUE)
sample_dists <- dist(t(assay(rld)))
sample_dist_matrix <- as.matrix(sample_dists)
rownames(sample_dist_matrix) <- paste(colData(rld)$Treatment, colData(rld)$Replicate, sep = "-")
colnames(sample_dist_matrix) <- rownames(sample_dist_matrix)

png(file.path(output_dir, "sample_distance_heatmap.png"), width = 1800, height = 1600, res = 300, bg = "transparent")
pheatmap(sample_dist_matrix, clustering_distance_rows = sample_dists, clustering_distance_cols = sample_dists, col = colorRampPalette(rev(brewer.pal(9, "Blues")))(255))
dev.off()
```

```{r dds-run}
dds <- DESeq(dds)
write.csv(as.data.frame(sizeFactors(dds)), file.path(output_dir, "DESeq2_size_factors.csv"))
```

```{r results}
res <- results(dds, contrast = c("Treatment", contrast_group, baseline))
res_df <- as.data.frame(res)
res_df$gene_id <- rownames(res_df)
res_df <- res_df[order(res_df$padj, na.last = TRUE), ]
write.csv(res_df, file.path(output_dir, paste0("DESeq2_", contrast_group, "_vs_", baseline, ".csv")), row.names = FALSE)
```

```{r ma-plot}
res_df$significant <- ifelse(!is.na(res_df$padj) & res_df$padj < 0.05, "Significant", "Not significant")
ma_plot <- ggplot(res_df, aes(x = baseMean, y = log2FoldChange, color = significant)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Significant" = ma_sig_color, "Not significant" = ma_nonsig_color)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  theme_classic() +
  transparent_theme

ggsave(file.path(output_dir, "MA_plot.png"), ma_plot, width = 6, height = 5, dpi = 300, bg = "transparent")
ma_plot
```

```{r volcano-plot}
res_df$threshold <- "Not significant"
res_df$threshold[!is.na(res_df$padj) & res_df$padj < 0.05 & res_df$log2FoldChange > 1] <- "Upregulated"
res_df$threshold[!is.na(res_df$padj) & res_df$padj < 0.05 & res_df$log2FoldChange < -1] <- "Downregulated"

volcano_plot <- ggplot(res_df, aes(x = log2FoldChange, y = -log10(padj), color = threshold)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 1.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1, 1), linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(color = "Direction") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Upregulated" = volcano_up_color,
    "Downregulated" = volcano_down_color,
    "Not significant" = volcano_nonsig_color
  )) +
  theme_classic() +
  transparent_theme

ggsave(file.path(output_dir, "Volcano_plot.png"), volcano_plot, width = 6, height = 5, dpi = 600, bg = "transparent")
volcano_plot
```

```{r deg-heatmap}
sig_genes <- head(res_df$gene_id[!is.na(res_df$padj) & res_df$padj < 0.05], 50)
if (length(sig_genes) > 1) {
  deg_mat <- assay(vst(dds, blind = FALSE))[sig_genes, , drop = FALSE]
  deg_mat <- t(scale(t(deg_mat)))
  deg_mat[is.na(deg_mat)] <- 0
  pdf(file.path(output_dir, "DEG_heatmap.pdf"), width = 8, height = 10)
  print(Heatmap(deg_mat, name = "Z-score", show_row_names = TRUE, show_column_names = TRUE, cluster_rows = TRUE, cluster_columns = TRUE))
  dev.off()
}
```

```{r}
gene_of_interest <- "TRINITY_DN18805_c0_g1_i23"
```

```{r gene-barplot}
if (nzchar(gene_of_interest)) {
  gene_plot_dir <- file.path(output_dir, "gene_barplots")
  dir.create(gene_plot_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

  sample_id <- function(path) sub("_quant\\.sf$", "", basename(path))
  gene_tpm <- do.call(
    rbind,
    lapply(quant_files, function(path) {
      dat <- read.delim(path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE)
      hit <- dat[dat$Name == gene_of_interest, , drop = FALSE]
      data.frame(
        Sample = sample_id(path),
        TPM = if (nrow(hit) == 0) NA_real_ else hit$TPM[1],
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      )
    })
  )

  gene_tpm$Group <- sub("[0-9]+$", "", gene_tpm$Sample)
  gene_tpm$log2_tpm <- log2(gene_tpm$TPM + 1)

  gene_summary <- aggregate(
    log2_tpm ~ Group,
    data = gene_tpm,
    FUN = function(x) c(mean = mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), sd = sd(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  )

  gene_summary <- data.frame(
    Group = gene_summary$Group,
    mean_log2_tpm = gene_summary$log2_tpm[, "mean"],
    sd_log2_tpm = gene_summary$log2_tpm[, "sd"]
  )
  gene_summary$Group <- factor(gene_summary$Group, levels = gene_summary$Group)
  cap_df <- data.frame(
    x = seq_along(gene_summary$Group),
    y = gene_summary$mean_log2_tpm + gene_summary$sd_log2_tpm
  )

  gene_plot <- ggplot(gene_summary, aes(x = Group, y = mean_log2_tpm)) +
    geom_col(fill = "black", width = 0.7) +
    geom_segment(aes(x = Group, xend = Group, y = mean_log2_tpm, yend = mean_log2_tpm + sd_log2_tpm)) +
    geom_segment(
      data = cap_df,
      aes(x = x - 0.08, xend = x + 0.08, y = y, yend = y),
      inherit.aes = FALSE,
      linewidth = 0.3
    ) +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, max(gene_summary$mean_log2_tpm + gene_summary$sd_log2_tpm, na.rm = TRUE))) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, Inf), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(title = paste0("Transcript abundance of ", gene_of_interest), x = NULL, y = "Average log2(TPM + 1)") +
    transparent_theme

  ggsave(
    file.path(gene_plot_dir, paste0("gene_", gene_of_interest, "_barplot.png")),
    gene_plot,
    width = 6,
    height = 5,
    dpi = 300,
    bg = "transparent"
  )
}
```
EOF

9.5 Render the downstream analysis

Principle: Rendering the R Markdown file executes each R chunk in order and creates an HTML report together with the exported tables and figures.

cd transcriptome_project

Rscript -e "if (!requireNamespace('rmarkdown', quietly = TRUE)) install.packages('rmarkdown', repos = 'https://cloud.r-project.org')"
Rscript -e "rmarkdown::render('scripts/downstream.Rmd', output_file = 'transcriptome_downstream_analysis.html', output_dir = 'downstream/trinity')"
Check
  • downstream/trinity/transcriptome_downstream_analysis.html
  • downstream/trinity/DESeq2_conditionB_vs_conditionA.csv
  • downstream/trinity/DESeq2_size_factors.csv
  • downstream/trinity/PCA_plot.png
  • downstream/trinity/sample_distance_heatmap.png
  • downstream/trinity/MA_plot.png
  • downstream/trinity/Volcano_plot.png
  • downstream/trinity/DEG_heatmap.pdf, produced only if at least two significant genes are detected
  • downstream/trinity/gene_barplots/gene_TRINITY_DN18805_c0_g1_i23_barplot.png, optional gene-level TPM summary plot

9.6 Downstream R chunks

Principle: The commands below are the downstream analysis chunks shown in the order they are executed in the R Markdown report.

9.6.1 Setup: install and load R packages; define paths and contrast settings

ensure_packages <- function(packages, bioc = character(0)) {
  missing_cran <- packages[!vapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE, FUN.VALUE = logical(1))]
  if (length(missing_cran) > 0) {
    install.packages(missing_cran, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
  }

  if (length(bioc) > 0) {
    if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
      install.packages("BiocManager", repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
    }
    missing_bioc <- bioc[!vapply(bioc, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE, FUN.VALUE = logical(1))]
    if (length(missing_bioc) > 0) {
      BiocManager::install(missing_bioc, ask = FALSE, update = FALSE)
    }
  }
}

ensure_packages(
  packages = c("ggplot2", "pheatmap", "RColorBrewer", "circlize"),
  bioc = c("DESeq2", "ComplexHeatmap")
)

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(DESeq2)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(pheatmap)
  library(RColorBrewer)
  library(ComplexHeatmap)
  library(circlize)
})

count_matrix_path <- "salmon/deseq_inputs/count_matrix.csv"
metadata_path <- "salmon/deseq_inputs/metadata.csv"
output_dir <- "downstream/trinity"
baseline <- "conditionA"
contrast_group <- "conditionB"
salmon_root <- "salmon"
ma_sig_color <- "darkred"
ma_nonsig_color <- "gray50"
volcano_up_color <- "darkblue"
volcano_down_color <- "darkred"
volcano_nonsig_color <- "gray50"

9.6.2 Initialize output folders and locate Salmon quantification files

dir.create(output_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
quant_files <- list.files(salmon_root, pattern = "_quant\\.sf$", full.names = TRUE)
stopifnot(length(quant_files) > 0)

9.6.3 Input: read count matrix and metadata

counts <- read.csv(count_matrix_path, row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)
metadata <- read.csv(metadata_path, row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)

counts <- round(as.matrix(counts))
storage.mode(counts) <- "integer"
metadata <- metadata[colnames(counts), , drop = FALSE]
metadata$Treatment <- factor(metadata$Treatment, levels = c(baseline, setdiff(unique(metadata$Treatment), baseline)))
stopifnot(all(colnames(counts) == rownames(metadata)))

9.6.4 Create DESeq2 dataset and filter low-count features

dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = counts, colData = metadata, design = ~ Treatment)
dds <- dds[rowSums(counts(dds) >= 10) >= min(table(metadata$Treatment)), ]
dds <- estimateSizeFactors(dds)
dds

9.6.5 PCA plot from variance-stabilized counts

vsd <- vst(dds, blind = TRUE)
pca_df <- plotPCA(vsd, intgroup = "Treatment", returnData = TRUE)
percent_var <- round(100 * attr(pca_df, "percentVar"))

pca_plot <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(PC1, PC2, color = Treatment, label = name)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(vjust = -0.8, size = 3) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "PCA", x = paste0("PC1: ", percent_var[1], "% variance"), y = paste0("PC2: ", percent_var[2], "% variance")) +
  transparent_theme

ggsave(file.path(output_dir, "PCA_plot.png"), pca_plot, width = 6, height = 5, dpi = 300, bg = "transparent")
pca_plot

Example output preview: downstream/trinity/PCA_plot.png

9.6.6 Sample distance heatmap

rld <- rlog(dds, blind = TRUE)
sample_dists <- dist(t(assay(rld)))
sample_dist_matrix <- as.matrix(sample_dists)
rownames(sample_dist_matrix) <- paste(colData(rld)$Treatment, colData(rld)$Replicate, sep = "-")
colnames(sample_dist_matrix) <- rownames(sample_dist_matrix)

png(file.path(output_dir, "sample_distance_heatmap.png"), width = 1800, height = 1600, res = 300, bg = "transparent")
pheatmap(sample_dist_matrix, clustering_distance_rows = sample_dists, clustering_distance_cols = sample_dists, col = colorRampPalette(rev(brewer.pal(9, "Blues")))(255))
dev.off()

Example output preview: downstream/trinity/sample_distance_heatmap.png

9.6.7 Run DESeq2 and export size factors

dds <- DESeq(dds)
write.csv(as.data.frame(sizeFactors(dds)), file.path(output_dir, "DESeq2_size_factors.csv"))

File preview: downstream/trinity/DESeq2_size_factors.csv

Sample sizeFactors(dds)
conditionA_1 1.09019
conditionA_2 1.06994
conditionA_3 0.866596
conditionB_1 1.10437
conditionB_2 0.930279
conditionB_3 1.15682

9.6.8 Extract contrast results and export differential-expression table

res <- results(dds, contrast = c("Treatment", contrast_group, baseline))
res_df <- as.data.frame(res)
res_df$gene_id <- rownames(res_df)
res_df <- res_df[order(res_df$padj, na.last = TRUE), ]
write.csv(res_df, file.path(output_dir, paste0("DESeq2_", contrast_group, "_vs_", baseline, ".csv")), row.names = FALSE)

File preview: downstream/trinity/DESeq2_conditionB_vs_conditionA.csv

baseMean log2FoldChange lfcSE stat pvalue padj gene_id
3097.38 -3.34576 0.126768 -26.3928 1.65555e-153 1.67829e-148 TRINITY_DN597_c3_g1_i1
3003.49 -6.59489 0.270772 -24.3559 5.02484e-131 2.54694e-126 TRINITY_DN106_c3_g1_i2
4708.87 1.75619 0.0748591 23.4599 1.04816e-121 3.54187e-117 TRINITY_DN4924_c0_g2_i6
1413.87 6.48769 0.283273 22.9026 4.3787e-116 1.10972e-111 TRINITY_DN1540_c2_g1_i1
7638.97 2.99235 0.134683 22.2177 2.31845e-109 4.70061e-105 TRINITY_DN6641_c0_g1_i26
719.74 -3.23363 0.145729 -22.1893 4.35863e-109 6.86818e-105 TRINITY_DN1637_c5_g1_i1

9.6.9 MA plot

res_df$significant <- ifelse(!is.na(res_df$padj) & res_df$padj < 0.05, "Significant", "Not significant")
ma_plot <- ggplot(res_df, aes(x = baseMean, y = log2FoldChange, color = significant)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Significant" = ma_sig_color, "Not significant" = ma_nonsig_color)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  theme_classic() +
  transparent_theme

ggsave(file.path(output_dir, "MA_plot.png"), ma_plot, width = 6, height = 5, dpi = 300, bg = "transparent")
ma_plot

Example output preview: downstream/trinity/MA_plot.png

9.6.10 Volcano plot

res_df$threshold <- "Not significant"
res_df$threshold[!is.na(res_df$padj) & res_df$padj < 0.05 & res_df$log2FoldChange > 1] <- "Upregulated"
res_df$threshold[!is.na(res_df$padj) & res_df$padj < 0.05 & res_df$log2FoldChange < -1] <- "Downregulated"

volcano_plot <- ggplot(res_df, aes(x = log2FoldChange, y = -log10(padj), color = threshold)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 1.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1, 1), linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(color = "Direction") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Upregulated" = volcano_up_color,
    "Downregulated" = volcano_down_color,
    "Not significant" = volcano_nonsig_color
  )) +
  theme_classic() +
  transparent_theme

ggsave(file.path(output_dir, "Volcano_plot.png"), volcano_plot, width = 6, height = 5, dpi = 600, bg = "transparent")
volcano_plot

Example output preview: downstream/trinity/Volcano_plot.png

9.6.11 Top significant DEG heatmap

sig_genes <- head(res_df$gene_id[!is.na(res_df$padj) & res_df$padj < 0.05], 50)
if (length(sig_genes) > 1) {
  deg_mat <- assay(vst(dds, blind = FALSE))[sig_genes, , drop = FALSE]
  deg_mat <- t(scale(t(deg_mat)))
  deg_mat[is.na(deg_mat)] <- 0
  pdf(file.path(output_dir, "DEG_heatmap.pdf"), width = 8, height = 10)
  print(Heatmap(deg_mat, name = "Z-score", show_row_names = TRUE, show_column_names = TRUE, cluster_rows = TRUE, cluster_columns = TRUE))
  dev.off()
}

Example output preview: downstream/trinity/DEG_heatmap.pdf

9.6.12 Set the gene of interest for optional TPM bar plot

gene_of_interest <- "TRINITY_DN18805_c0_g1_i23"

9.6.13 Gene-of-interest TPM bar plot

if (nzchar(gene_of_interest)) {
  gene_plot_dir <- file.path(output_dir, "gene_barplots")
  dir.create(gene_plot_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

  sample_id <- function(path) sub("_quant\\.sf$", "", basename(path))
  gene_tpm <- do.call(
    rbind,
    lapply(quant_files, function(path) {
      dat <- read.delim(path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE)
      hit <- dat[dat$Name == gene_of_interest, , drop = FALSE]
      data.frame(
        Sample = sample_id(path),
        TPM = if (nrow(hit) == 0) NA_real_ else hit$TPM[1],
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      )
    })
  )

  gene_tpm$Group <- sub("[0-9]+$", "", gene_tpm$Sample)
  gene_tpm$log2_tpm <- log2(gene_tpm$TPM + 1)

  gene_summary <- aggregate(
    log2_tpm ~ Group,
    data = gene_tpm,
    FUN = function(x) c(mean = mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), sd = sd(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  )

  gene_summary <- data.frame(
    Group = gene_summary$Group,
    mean_log2_tpm = gene_summary$log2_tpm[, "mean"],
    sd_log2_tpm = gene_summary$log2_tpm[, "sd"]
  )
  gene_summary$Group <- factor(gene_summary$Group, levels = gene_summary$Group)
  cap_df <- data.frame(
    x = seq_along(gene_summary$Group),
    y = gene_summary$mean_log2_tpm + gene_summary$sd_log2_tpm
  )

  gene_plot <- ggplot(gene_summary, aes(x = Group, y = mean_log2_tpm)) +
    geom_col(fill = "black", width = 0.7) +
    geom_segment(aes(x = Group, xend = Group, y = mean_log2_tpm, yend = mean_log2_tpm + sd_log2_tpm)) +
    geom_segment(
      data = cap_df,
      aes(x = x - 0.08, xend = x + 0.08, y = y, yend = y),
      inherit.aes = FALSE,
      linewidth = 0.3
    ) +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, max(gene_summary$mean_log2_tpm + gene_summary$sd_log2_tpm, na.rm = TRUE))) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, Inf), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(title = paste0("Transcript abundance of ", gene_of_interest), x = NULL, y = "Average log2(TPM + 1)") +
    transparent_theme

  ggsave(
    file.path(gene_plot_dir, paste0("gene_", gene_of_interest, "_barplot.png")),
    gene_plot,
    width = 6,
    height = 5,
    dpi = 300,
    bg = "transparent"
  )
}

Example output preview: downstream/trinity/gene_barplots/gene_TRINITY_DN18805_c0_g1_i23_barplot.png

9.7 Guide questions

Reflect
Why copy Salmon outputs into salmon/<sample>_quant.sf files?

The converter script is intentionally simple for training use. It searches only the top level of the salmon/ folder for files ending in _quant.sf.

Why use NumReads instead of TPM for DESeq2?

DESeq2 models count data. TPM values are already normalized for transcript length and library composition, so they are not the correct direct input for standard DESeq2 differential-expression testing.

What should be changed for a different comparison?

Edit baseline and contrast_group in the setup chunk of scripts/downstream.Rmd. For example, set baseline <- "conditionA" and contrast_group <- "conditionB" to compute conditionB versus conditionA.

10

Backup Output Downloads

Fallback files: If a step fails or takes too long during the session, use the prepared output files to continue the tutorial.

Download: Open the shared Google Drive folder for all output files.

Files are provided in one folder — check file names and place them in the expected project directories before continuing.

Glossary

Key terms used in this module.

RNA-seq — High-throughput sequencing of RNA to profile transcripts and expression.
Transcriptome — Complete set of RNA transcripts expressed in a sample.
De novo assembly — Reconstructing transcript sequences from reads without a reference genome.
FASTQ — Standard format for raw or processed sequencing reads and quality scores.
Conda environment — Isolated software environment for installing and activating bioinformatics tools.
FastQC / MultiQC — Read QC tool and report aggregator used before and after preprocessing.
fastp — All-in-one tool for adapter removal, quality trimming, and QC reporting.
Adapter trimming — Removing adapter sequences and low-quality bases from reads.
Rcorrector — Optional k-mer–based error correction before trimming.
SortMeRNA — Optional tool filtering ribosomal RNA reads using SILVA references.
Trinity — De novo assembler reconstructing transcripts from cleaned paired-end reads.
CD-HIT-EST — Clusters similar transcripts to reduce redundancy after assembly.
N50 — Assembly metric: length at which half of total assembled bases are in longer contigs/transcripts.
TransDecoder — Predicts coding regions (ORFs) from assembled transcripts.
Open reading frame (ORF) — Contiguous region that could encode a protein.
Salmon — Quantifies transcript abundance by assigning reads to transcript sequences.
TPM — Transcripts per million—length-normalized abundance for comparing expression.
quant.sf — Salmon output table with transcript names, lengths, TPM, and counts.
Trinotate — Pipeline integrating BLAST, Pfam, and database hits for functional annotation.
DIAMOND — Fast aligner for BLAST-like protein and translated nucleotide searches.
Gene Ontology (GO) — Vocabulary of biological functions, processes, and components.
Reference-based workflow — Optional path mapping reads to a known genome with HISAT2 and StringTie.
HISAT2 — Splice-aware aligner mapping RNA-seq reads to a reference genome.
BAM — Binary format storing mapped reads with genomic coordinates.
StringTie — Reference-guided assembler building transcript models from aligned BAM files.
GTF — File format describing genomic coordinates of genes, transcripts, and exons.
Count matrix — Samples-by-transcripts table of integer counts for DESeq2 input.
Sample metadata — Table of sample IDs, treatments, and replicates matching count matrix columns.
DESeq2 — R package for differential expression analysis from count data.
log2FoldChange — Log₂-scaled fold change in expression between conditions.
padj — Multiple-testing–adjusted p-value for judging statistical significance.
DEG — Differentially expressed gene/transcript (e.g., padj < 0.05).
PCA / MA plot / Volcano plot — Exploratory and summary plots for expression patterns and DE results.
R Markdown (`.Rmd`) — Document combining R code and narrative, rendered to HTML for downstream analysis.
End of tutorial module. This version includes Salmon-to-DESeq2 conversion and downstream DESeq2 analysis after the optional reference-based workflow.
